questions.question: Explain the difference between user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Mention one advantage and one disadvantage of each.
keyPoints: User-level threads are managed by a library in user space without kernel involvement
Kernel-level threads are managed directly by the operating system kernel
User-level threads are fast (no kernel calls) but cannot achieve true parallelism on multiprocessors
Kernel-level threads can utilize multiple processors but have higher overhead due to kernel involvement

questions.question: What is a thread library? List any two popular thread libraries and their operating systems.
keyPoints: Thread library provides API for creating and managing threads
POSIX Pthreads - used in UNIX/Linux systems
Win32 Threads - native to Windows
Java Threads - cross-platform using JVM

questions.question: Describe the pthread_create() function and its parameters.
keyPoints: pthread_create() creates a new thread
Parameters: thread (output), attributes, start_routine, argument
Returns 0 on success, error number on failure
The start_routine is the function that the new thread will execute

questions.question: What is a mutex? How does it differ from a semaphore?
keyPoints: Mutex provides mutual exclusion - only one thread can access resource at a time
Mutex is essentially a binary semaphore (0 or 1)
Semaphores can allow multiple simultaneous accesses (counting semaphores)
Mutex ownership is only with the thread that locks it; semaphores have no ownership concept

questions.question: Explain the many-to-one thread model. What is its main limitation?
keyPoints: Many-to-one maps multiple user threads to a single kernel thread
Thread management is done by user-level library
If any user thread blocks (e.g., for I/O), the entire process blocks
Cannot achieve true parallelism on multiprocessor systems - major limitation

questions.question: What is a thread pool? Why is it used in multithreaded applications?
keyPoints: Thread pool is a collection of pre-created reusable worker threads
Reduces overhead of creating new threads for each task
Improves response time and system resource utilization
Tasks are submitted to the pool and executed by available threads

questions.question: Describe the purpose of condition variables in thread synchronization.
keyPoints: Condition variables allow threads to wait for certain conditions to become true
Used with mutexes for complex synchronization patterns
Provides wait(), signal(), and broadcast() operations
Useful for producer-consumer and reader-writer problems

questions.question: Explain the three main thread models: many-to-one, one-to-one, and many-to-many. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Many-to-one: multiple user threads to single kernel thread - efficient but no parallelism
One-to-one: each user thread to kernel thread - true parallelism but higher overhead
Many-to-many: multiplex user threads to equal/lesser kernel threads - balanced approach
Comparison should include: parallelism capability, overhead, scalability, blocking behavior

questions.question: Write a Pthreads program to create multiple threads that compute the sum of elements in an array. Explain the thread creation and synchronization mechanisms used.
keyPoints: Use pthread_create() to spawn worker threads
Each thread computes sum of assigned portion of array
Use pthread_join() to wait for all threads and collect results
Show proper error handling
Explain how mutex could be used if threads write to shared variable

questions.question: Explain the producer-consumer problem. How would you solve it using mutex and condition variables in Pthreads?
keyPoints: Producer creates items and consumer consumes items
Shared buffer with limited capacity
Producer waits when buffer is full
Consumer waits when buffer is empty
Use mutex for mutual exclusion on buffer access
Use two condition variables: one for buffer not full, one for buffer not empty
Show example code structure with proper synchronization

questions.question: Compare user-level threads and kernel-level threads. Under what circumstances would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: User-level: managed by user library, faster creation/switching, no kernel involvement
Kernel-level: managed by OS, can utilize multiple CPUs, slower due to system calls
Prefer user-level when: thread management is lightweight, no true parallelism needed
Prefer kernel-level when: compute-intensive tasks need parallelism, blocking I/O is involved
Hybrid approaches combine benefits of both
